briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. The climate briefing for July 9, 2026, will now commence.

sections.title: 🔴 Climate Change: Severe Impacts on Domestic and International Ecosystems and Societies
sections.content: Today's major domestic and international climate news is filled with warnings about the direct and severe impacts of climate change on ecosystems and our lives. The 'era of live tuna,' where tuna are caught in schools in the East Sea, illustrates a facet of marine ecosystem change [1], and the northward migration of Arctic fishing grounds has also been presented as clear evidence of climate change [2]. The domestic beekeeping industry is experiencing the severity of climate change firsthand, facing difficulties with migratory beekeeping alongside warnings from bees [3, 6]. Globally, extreme weather phenomena are frequently observed, such as record-breaking heatwaves and marine heatwaves striking Europe, causing casualties and changes in working environments [8, 85, 86]. Furthermore, research results have also reported that specific species, such as the northern tree swallow [51], are becoming vulnerable due to climate change, as are the plants and cultures of the Amazon [52, 60, 69].

sections.title: ⚠️ Economic Risks and Policy Challenges Posed by Climate Change
sections.content: Climate change is causing immense risks and new policy challenges across the economy. Concerns have been raised that Italy's GDP could decrease by up to 6% by 2050 due to the direct impact of the climate crisis, with long-term economic growth hindrance and worsening debt risks anticipated [9, 57, 72, 75]. The deepening crisis of urban water bill affordability and the potential for individual water bill increases have also been mentioned [47, 50, 59], and concerns are growing about the increased burden of uninsured costs for homeowners due to extreme weather [82]. Meanwhile, social debates and resistance surrounding climate policies are also becoming visible, with New Zealand pushing to ban climate change lawsuits, and some farmer groups protesting government decrees related to renewable energy facilities, criticizing that they are 'turning rural areas into renewable energy colonies' [33, 45]. Conflicts during the energy transition process are also noteworthy, particularly with the owner of the Jackdaw gas field claiming that the field will not significantly impact climate change [43, 84].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Renewable Energy and Technological Innovations for Achieving Carbon Neutrality Goals
sections.content: Amidst these threats, domestic and international efforts and innovations to achieve carbon neutrality goals continue. Pohang City plans to usher in a carbon-neutral future through the 2026 World Green Growth Forum (WGGF) [14, 16, 18, 27], and positive progress is visible in various sectors, with Seoul Sanggye Paik Hospital and TH Group being selected as excellent companies for carbon-neutral activities [11, 13, 28]. Domestically, technological innovation is active, with Korea Hydro & Nuclear Power (KHNP) developing 'water electrolysis operation technology' that can accommodate renewable energy variability without ESS [30, 36], and LG Electronics commercializing high-temperature, large-capacity industrial heat pumps [22]. The Korea Power Exchange is strengthening its response to energy transition by establishing a dedicated renewable energy organization [31, 38], and institutional foundations are being laid to enhance corporate climate responsibility, such as mandating carbon emission disclosure for companies with assets exceeding 10 trillion won [42]. Internationally, the renewable energy market continues to expand, with solar power projects being sold in France and bids for renewable energy plant construction being pursued in Mongolia [39, 40].

sections.title: 🌍 Raising Social Awareness and Education for Climate Change Adaptation
sections.content: Efforts to raise social awareness and adapt to climate change are also unfolding in various forms. Domestically, Chungju City is operating a 'Climate Change Academy' for elementary school students [4, 7], and Gyeyang-gu is spreading a culture of carbon neutrality practice through visiting environmental education for community leaders [23]. The fisheries industry is exploring 'adaptation' and 'leap forward' beyond mere 'survival' amidst the immense wave of climate change, presenting new directions [5]. Local governments are promoting the establishment of dedicated carbon neutrality departments and the expansion of the greenhouse gas reduction budget awareness system [24]. Internationally, the Columbia Climate School and Sciences Po are striving to educate future generations by establishing joint degree programs focused on climate action [62]. In Canada, it has been observed that seniors aged 65 and over are more concerned about climate change than younger generations, indicating a shift in overall societal awareness [81]. In Europe, a multifaceted approach is being taken, with discussions extending beyond solar panels to new climate adaptation measures such as asphalt [89].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the severe reality of climate change and the complex domestic and international efforts to address it. It is crucial to overcome the climate crisis through continued attention and cooperation. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: Companies with Assets Exceeding 10 Trillion Won to Disclose 'Carbon Emissions' from 2028
articles.original_title: 자산 10조 넘는 기업, 2028년부터 ‘탄소 배출량’ 공시
articles.description: Final plan for institutionalizing ESG disclosure... Strengthened from initial draft of '30 trillion won', to over 5 trillion won in 2029. From 2028, companies with total assets, including subsidiaries, exceeding 10 trillion won must disclose climate-related indicators such as greenhouse gas emissions once a year. The Financial Services Commission announced the 'Plan for Institutionalizing Sustainability (ESG) Disclosure (Final Version)' at a government-party consultation held on the morning of the 8th. Discussions on introducing ESG disclosure began in 2021...

summary.phenomenon: The Financial Services Commission announced the 'Plan for Institutionalizing Sustainability (ESG) Disclosure (Final Version)' at a government-party consultation held on the morning of the 8th. Accordingly, from 2028, companies with total assets exceeding 10 trillion won must disclose climate-related indicators, such as greenhouse gas emissions, once a year, and in 2029, the scope will be expanded to include companies with total assets exceeding 5 trillion won.

articles.original_description: ESG 공시 제도화 최종안…초안 ‘30조’서 강화, 2029년엔 5조 이상으로2028년부터 자회사까지 모두 더한 총자산이 10조원을 넘는 기업은 온실가스 배출량과 같은 기후 관련 지표를 1년에 한 번 공시해야 한다.금융위원회는 8일 오전 열린 당정협의회에서 ‘지속 가능성(ESG) 공시 제도화 방안(최종안)’을 발표했다. 2021년 ESG 공시 도입 논의가 ···

articles.original_title: UK waters hit with extreme heatwave as global sea temperatures reach record levels
articles.description: Experts warn that some marine species are at risk of ‘mass mortality events’ in ever-warming oceansUK waters are being hit with an “extreme” marine heatwave, the Met Office has said, as scientists warn that high ocean temperatures globally could result in “mass-mortality events” for some species.The forecasters said these elevated temperatures have developed rapidly because of last month’s heat dome, during which most of Europe sweltered in its worst ever heatwave that scientists said would have

summary.phenomenon: UK waters are experiencing an 'extreme' marine heatwave, and global ocean temperatures have reached record levels.
summary.cause: Ocean temperatures have risen rapidly due to the 'heat dome' phenomenon that caused the worst heatwave to hit most of Europe last month.
summary.outlook: Experts warn that some marine species are at risk of 'mass mortality events' in ever-warming oceans.

articles.original_title: Unions in Europe press for new worker protections to counter heat stress
articles.description: Climate crisis prompts calls for workplace temperature limits and rights to heat breaks and adjusted working hoursAs Europe’s sweltering summer continues, trades unions are mounting a push for new laws to counter deadly heat stress that is linked to an estimated 230 workplace deaths a year.This year’s toll may be even higher, with 1,300 excess European deaths already connected to the June heatwave by the World Health Organization, and other estimates running as high as 20,000. Continue reading..

summary.phenomenon: As Europe's sweltering summer continues, trade unions are pushing for new legislation to counter deadly heat stress linked to an estimated 230 workplace deaths annually. The World Health Organization (WHO) has already linked 1,300 excess European deaths to the June heatwave, with other estimates reaching as high as 20,000.
summary.cause: The climate crisis is causing workplace heat stress, leading to fatalities.
summary.outlook: European trade unions will push for new worker protection legislation, including workplace temperature limits, rights to heat breaks, and adjusted working hours.

articles.original_description: Climate crisis prompts calls for workplace temperature limits and rights to heat breaks and adjusted working hoursAs Europe’s sweltering summer continues, trades unions are mounting a push for new laws to counter deadly heat stress that is linked to an estimated 230 workplace deaths a year.This year’s toll may be even higher, with 1,300 excess European deaths already connected to the June heatwave by the World Health Organization, and other estimates running as high as 20,000. Continue reading..

articles.title: European countries top ‘scorecard’ on climate progress while US slips to 27th
articles.description: Estonia, Luxembourg and UK are the top three in biennial Yale University index in tackling pollution and other issuesMuch of the world has made encouraging strides in reducing toxic problems such as water and air pollution that have long plagued communities. But there is still a widespread lack of progress among countries in dealing with the climate crisis, according to the latest edition of an influential environmental scorecard.The biennial Yale University index again ranks Estonia as the best

summary.phenomenon: According to Yale University's biennial Environmental Performance Index, Estonia, Luxembourg, and the UK ranked in the top three for tackling climate issues, while the US dropped to 27th place.
summary.outlook: Globally, there has been progress in reducing harmful issues such as water and air pollution, but a widespread lack of progress in addressing the climate crisis persists.

articles.title: 데이터센터는 시한폭탄이다. AI의 이점이 비용을 상회하는지 확인해야 한다.
articles.original_title: Datacentres are a ticking timebomb. We must make sure AI’s benefits outweigh the costs | Nicki Hutley
articles.description: They suck up energy and water, and blast out heat. Just who is better off from all this investment – aside from tech bros?The two great existential threats of our time – the climate crisis and AI – come hurtling together in the explosion of datacentres across Australia and around the world.You can hardly avoid hearing about them these days, either with awed reverence of the promised benefits to humankind or with fear and anger given the implications for the climate, inflation, jobs and even hous

summary.phenomenon: Data centers are rapidly increasing across Australia and globally, coinciding with the two existential threats of climate crisis and AI.
summary.cause: Because data centers consume large amounts of energy and water, and emit heat.
summary.outlook: The surge in data centers could impact various sectors including climate, inflation, jobs, and housing, and it needs to be determined if the benefits of AI outweigh these costs.
